quality.rationale: 基于旋转群生成元与基灵条件进行多步张量约束推导，逻辑链条完整，为球对称时空（如Schwarzschild度规）提供严格的理论基础

origin.question: Problem 6.10. Find the most general form for a spacetime metric that is (spatially) spherically symmetric.
origin.solution:
## Solution

Solution 6.10. A geometrical object exhibits a particular group symmetry if its functional change (i.e. its change in functional form) under the action of the group vanishes. If $\xi$ is an infinitesimal displacement, then under the infinitesimal coordinate transformation $\mathbf{x}'^{\mu} = \mathbf{x}^{\mu} + \xi^{\mu}$ , one can easily show that the functional change of the metric, $\overline{{\delta}}\mathbf{g}_{\mu 
u}$ , is $$ \overline {{{\delta}}} \mathbf {g}_{\mu 
u} \equiv \mathbf {g}_{\mu 
u}^{\prime} (\mathbf {x}) - \mathbf {g}_{\mu 
u} (\mathbf {x}) = - \mathbf {g}_{\mu ho} \xi_{, 
u}^{ho} - \mathbf {g}_{ho 
u} \xi_{, \mu}^{ eta} - \mathbf {g}_{\mu 
u , ho} \xi^{ho}. \qquad (1) $$ (See Problem 13.12.) For spherical symmetry, $\overline{{\delta}}\mathbf{g}_{\mu 
u}$ must vanish under the rotation group, a particular realization of which is obtained from the generators $$ \xi^{0} = 0, \quad \xi^{\mathrm {i}} = \varepsilon^{\mathrm {i j}} \mathbf {x}^{\mathrm {j}}, 	ag {2} $$ where $\varepsilon^{\mathrm{ij}} = -\varepsilon^{\mathrm{ji}}$ are three arbitrary infinitesimal constants. Substituting Equation (2) for the generators $\xi^{\mu}$ in Equation (1) and setting the left side to zero, one gets: a. $\mu = 
u = 0$ $$ \mathbf {g}_{0 0,\mathrm {i}} \varepsilon^{\mathrm {i j}} \mathbf {x}^{\mathrm {j}} = 0 \quad 	ext {o r} \quad \mathbf {g}_{0 0,\mathrm {i}} \mathbf {x}^{\mathrm {j}} = \mathbf {g}_{0 0,\mathrm {j}} \mathbf {x}^{\mathrm {i}} $$ and hence $$  egin{array}{l} \mathbf {g}_{0 0} = \mathbf {g}_{0 0}(\mathbf {x}^{0}, \mathbf {r}^{2}) \ r^{2} \equiv (x^{1})^{2} + (x^{2})^{2} + (x^{3})^{2}. 	ag {3a} \ \end{array} $$ b. $\mu = 0, 
u 
eq 0$ $$  egin{array}{l} \mathbf {g}_{0 \mathrm {i}} \varepsilon^{\mathrm {i j}} + \mathbf {g}_{0 \mathrm {j},\mathrm {i}} \varepsilon^{\mathrm {i k}} \mathbf {x}^{\mathrm {k}} = 0 \ \mathrm {g}_{0 \mathrm {j}} = \Gamma_{1}(\mathrm {r}^{2}, \mathrm {x}^{0}) \mathrm {x}^{\mathrm {j}} 	ag {3b} \ \end{array} $$ where $\Gamma_{1}$ is an arbitrary function. c. $\mu 
eq 0$ ， $
u 
eq 0$ $$  egin{array}{l} \mathbf {g}_{\mathbf {i k}} \varepsilon^{\mathbf {k j}} + \mathbf {g}_{\mathbf {\ell j}} \varepsilon^{\mathbf {\ell i}} + \mathbf {g}_{\mathbf {i j},\ell} \varepsilon^{\mathbf {\ell k}} \mathbf {x}^{\mathbf {k}} = 0 \ \mathbf {g}_{\mathbf {i j}} = \Gamma_{2}(\mathrm {r}^{2}, \mathrm {x}^{0}) \delta_{\mathbf {i j}} + \Gamma_{3}(\mathrm {r}^{2}, \mathrm {x}^{0}) \mathrm {x}^{\mathrm {i}} \mathrm {x}^{\mathrm {j}} 	ag {3c} \ \end{array} $$ where $\Gamma_{2}$ and $\Gamma_{3}$ are again arbitrary functions. From Equation (2) one can see that the above coordinate system $(\mathbf{x}^0, \mathbf{x}^1, \mathbf{x}^2, \mathbf{x}^3)$ is “cartesian-like.” The group theoretically defined property of spherical symmetry is coordinate independent, however, and to find $\mathbf{g}_{\mu 
u}$ in any other coordinate system one merely transforms the metric given in Equations (3) in the usual way.
